Part One The Opportunity
01
Chapter One

The Market Reality:
Where We Stand and Why It Must Change

For five years, Smith+Nephew's hip market share in FraBeNe has been in structural decline while the market itself grows. The data tells a clear story — and it demands a clear response.

11%
Hip market share in 2020
5.9%
Hip market share by Q3 2025
74%
Current revenue from knees
−6.1%
S+N hip growth vs +5.7% market

The market is growing. Our share is declining. The gap between those two facts is not a problem to be managed — it is an opportunity to be seized.

Power of One — Strategic Foundation

The FraBeNe hip replacement market grew at 5.7% in 2025. Smith+Nephew's hip business in the same period contracted by 6.1%. Our market share has fallen from approximately 11% in 2020 to below 6% today. Meanwhile, our revenue portfolio remains 74% knees and 26% hips — a structural imbalance that leaves us over-reliant on one franchise and under-invested in a growing one.

Power of One is the deliberate, structured response to this reality. It is a commitment to hip mastery across every territory, every representative, and every account in FraBeNe — not as an aspiration, but as the defining objective of how we work.

What we must leave behind
✕
Competing half-heartedly
Letting hips remain secondary to knees in mindshare, resource allocation, and rep confidence.
✕
Delegating mastery
Relying on a handful of specialists while the majority of reps lack confidence in hip conversations.
✕
Waiting for the fix
Assuming the market or leadership will correct the trajectory without individual ownership and action.
02
Chapter Two

Campaign Architecture:
Power of One — 2026 Phasing

Power of One is a structured, multi-phase programme designed to build intelligence, expand brand presence, and systematically convert accounts across FraBeNe. The playbook is now finalised and the first conversion sprint launches in May 2026.

Activation — Complete
January 27 – 31, 2026
IKOM — IGNITE 2026
Team activation, training kickoff, breakout sessions on POLAR3 design, clinical data, selling strategy and RI HIP robotics. Foundation for all subsequent phases established.
Phase 1 · Intelligence & Targeting — Complete
February – April 2026
Voice of Customer Studios & Playbook Development
Structured surgeon interviews conducted (20 min, recorded, AI-analysed). 5 interviews per rep across FraBeNe. Focus: current practice, implant decision-making, barriers to adoption, S+N perception. Output: customised account playbooks, pipeline database, barrier maps. Playbook now finalised and ready for field deployment.
Phase 2 · Conversion Sprint 1
May – August 2026
Brand Expansion
Full playbook execution across all territories. Pipeline management, local account engagement, trial bookings and VSP development. Monthly wins recognition and knowledge sharing calls. This sprint is the primary commercial engine of the campaign — every rep is expected to be actively executing their account playbook from day one of May.
Leadership Engagement
Second Half 2026
Leadership Roadshow — Key Account Visits
In place of a large congress format, leadership will conduct a targeted roadshow visiting key accounts directly across France, Belgium and the Netherlands. Designed to demonstrate organisational commitment at the highest level, support account conversion, secure surgeon commitments, and engage KOLs in their own environment. Account selection will be driven by Sprint 1 pipeline intelligence.
Phase 3 · Conversion Sprint 2
Q4 2026 – Q1 2027
Customer Expansion
Playbook repositioning based on Sprint 1 learnings, database rebuilding, intelligence gathering on new accounts. Sustained conversion through end of FY and into early 2027.
The Power of One — Key Themes
1:1
One Vision
A 1:1 hip-to-knee revenue ratio across the FraBeNe cluster by 2030. Not a target to be tracked quarterly — a commitment that reshapes how every rep allocates their day.
€20M
One Goal
€20M in hip revenue by 2030. The market is growing at 5.7%. Our market share is declining. The gap represents both the problem and the opportunity.
ALL
One Team
Hip mastery is not the responsibility of specialists. Every territory, every representative, every account. No more delegating. No more averaging.
R3
One Solution
POLAR3 — POLARSTEM with the R3 Cup. One system for all morphologies. One instrumentation set for cemented and uncemented. One training. One outcome story.
Part Two The POLAR3 System
03
Chapter Three

POLARSTEM:
Engineered for Every Morphology

A cementless triple-taper femoral stem system built around one core philosophy — reliable primary stability, rapid osseointegration, and versatility that eliminates compromise across the full range of patient anatomies.

Indications

POLARSTEM is indicated for advanced hip degeneration from degenerative, post-traumatic or rheumatoid arthritis; fracture or avascular necrosis of the femoral head; and failure of previous hip surgery including joint reconstruction, internal fixation, hemiarthroplasty, surface replacement arthroplasty, or total hip replacement.

Available in both cementless (Ti/HA) and cemented configurations to accommodate the full spectrum of surgical preference and patient anatomy.

System Options
VariantNeck AngleSizes
Standard (Press-fit)135°0 – 11
Lateral (Press-fit)126°1 – 11
Valgus (Press-fit)145°0 – 7
Standard Collared135°0 – 11
Lateral Collared126°1 – 11
Cemented Standard135°0 – 8
Cemented Lateral126°1 – 8

Material: Ti-6Al-4V (press-fit) / Stainless Steel (cemented)

Key Design Features
01
Triple Taper Geometry
7° coronal taper combined with 8° anteversion and a 3.2° sagittal taper creates a self-locking press-fit that maximises primary stability across the full range of femoral morphologies.
02
Advanced Ti/HA Coating
180µm open-porous Titanium Plasma Spray (VPS process) topped with a 50µm HA layer — the optimal balance for primary fixation, osseointegration, and minimised delamination risk.
03
Reinforced Proximal Body
Proximal diameter approximately 20% wider than CORAIL with a greater proximal taper angle — enhancing proximal loading and reducing subsidence risk without compromising distal flexibility.
04
Medial Cortex Alignment
The medial margin of the stem aligns to the medial cortex, resisting subsidence and ensuring consistent leg length restoration. Rasps carry bone-cutting teeth medial/lateral for precise cortical contact.
05
Consistent Neck Geometry
Neck length remains constant for sizes 1–10, simplifying templating and intraoperative decision-making. Stems grow 1.3mm laterally and 4mm in length per size, enabling precise anatomical reconstruction.
06
Multi-Approach Instrumentation
Full instrumentation compatibility across posterior, lateral, and direct anterior approach (DAA) with dedicated offset adaptors and a curved impactor. One training requirement for the full portfolio.
04
Chapter Four

The R3 Acetabular System:
Completing the POLAR3 Construct

The R3 is not simply the cup that pairs with POLARSTEM — it is the component that unlocks the full clinical and commercial story of POLAR3. ODEP 13A★ rated, with the most comprehensive bearing matrix in the portfolio and a shell design engineered to protect every liner option across the patient's lifetime.

Shell Specifications
ConfigurationSize Range
No Hole Shell40 – 68 mm
3 Hole Shell40 – 68 mm
Multi-Hole Shell (8 holes)48 – 52 mm
Multi-Hole Shell (10 holes)54 – 62 mm
Multi-Hole Shell (12 holes)64 – 68 mm
Multi-Hole Shell (14 holes)70 – 80 mm

Material: Titanium Alloy (Ti-6Al-4V) · ODEP Rating: 13A★

Bearing Options
Bearing CoupleNote
OXINIUM on XLPERecommended — lowest revision risk
BIOLOX delta on XLPECeramic head option
CoCr on XLPEStandard metal head
BIOLOX delta on BIOLOX deltaCeramic-on-ceramic
OR3ODual mobility construct
XLPE Liner Options
Standard 0° Liner
Straight neutral orientation for standard anatomy and well-positioned cups.
20° Anteverted Liner
Adds 20° of anteversion to reduce posterior dislocation risk, particularly useful in posterior approach cases.
0° +4mm Lateralized Liner
Offsets the hip centre laterally by 4mm to optimise soft tissue tension and hip stability without changing cup position.
20° Anteverted +4mm Lateralized
Combines both adjustments — maximum versatility for complex cases where both anteversion and lateralisation are needed.
Shell Design: Built to Protect the Liner
01
STIKTITE◊ Coating
Asymmetric 3D porous bead structure, 60% porosity, with a high coefficient of friction. Delivers immediate primary stability at impaction and a robust biological fixation surface for long-term osseointegration.
02
12 Anti-Rotation Tabs
Robust tab system locks the liner circumferentially to prevent spin-out — a critical protection mechanism especially at high flexion angles or in high-demand patients.
03
Medialized Locking Mechanism
The locking mechanism is positioned medially (inside the cup rim) rather than at the rim itself. This reduces soft tissue impingement risk and distributes stress over a greater surface area.
04
Flush Liner Seating
The XLPE liner sits flush with the shell rim — reducing impingement loading on the liner edge and maximising range of motion (ROM) without compromising stability.
05
Ultra-Congruency
The head-to-shell geometry ratio is optimised for maximum contact area, distributing load evenly across the bearing surface and reducing peak stress concentrations over the implant's lifetime.
06
XLPE Quality: 10 MRad
The highly cross-linked polyethylene is irradiated to 10 MRad and remelted to eliminate free radicals — maximising wear resistance while preventing oxidative degradation over time.

The R3 is the only acetabular cup that combines ODEP 13A★ registry survivorship with access to OXINIUM bearing technology — a combination no competitor can replicate.

POLAR3 Clinical & Commercial Positioning
05
Chapter Five

OXINIUM:
The Bearing That Changes the Conversation

Every time a surgeon uses POLAR3, they have access to OXINIUM — a bearing technology that delivers the lowest risk of revision in primary THA across four national and regional joint registries, outperforming both ceramic-on-XLPE and ceramic-on-ceramic. No competitor can offer this. This is a unique differentiator that belongs in every sales conversation.

Revision Risk — 4 National Registries
0.1–1.7%
Revision risk with OXINIUM at 5–15 years across four national and regional joint registries. Lowest of any bearing surface analysed.
Brittle Fracture — OXINIUM with POLAR3
ZERO
Zero recorded instances of brittle fracture with OXINIUM Technology used with POLAR3. This is a risk that ceramic carries — OXINIUM eliminates it.
vs Ceramic — UK NJR Infection Data
↓ Inf.
OXINIUM displayed numerically lower incidence of revision for infection versus all other bearing surfaces in UK NJR data — a clinically significant differentiator.
Head Size & Offset Range
22–44mm
Greater range of head sizes (22–44mm) and offsets than ceramic — providing more intraoperative flexibility to reconstruct hip biomechanics precisely.
Why OXINIUM beats ceramic — the material science case
Equivalent Wear — Without the Risk
OXINIUM demonstrates equivalent wear properties to ceramic in vitro — so surgeons get the wear performance of ceramic without accepting its structural risks.
No Phase Transformation
Monolithic ceramic is susceptible to phase transformation over time — causing surface roughening and weakening. The ceramic oxide on OXINIUM does not transform in the same way, maintaining surface integrity long-term.
Lower Fretting & Corrosion
OXINIUM shows lower fretting and corrosion at the head-stem taper junction than both CoCr and ceramic heads — reducing the risk of adverse local tissue reactions over time.
No Sleeve Required for Head Exchange
Head exchanges onto existing POLARSTEM stems do not require a sleeve — avoiding the introduction of an additional junction and simplifying revision procedures.
No Squeaking
Noise generation affects 0–4.7% of ceramic-on-ceramic bearings and is a documented cause of patient dissatisfaction. OXINIUM on XLPE addresses this concern entirely — an argument surgeons' patients will care about.
Fracture Risk Eliminated
Ceramic brittle fracture occurs in approximately 0.11% of cases. While rare, the consequences are severe and revision is complex. OXINIUM removes this risk entirely from the equation.

OXINIUM is the only bearing available to POLAR3 users that has never fractured, outperforms ceramic on infection risk, and leads four independent registries on survivorship. No competitor system can offer this combination.

OXINIUM vs Ceramic — Smith+Nephew Clinical Evidence
The sales argument in one sentence
?
"If you could give your patient the wear performance of ceramic, eliminate fracture risk, reduce infection risk, and never worry about squeaking — at no additional clinical cost — why would you use anything else?"
Use this question to open the OXINIUM conversation with ceramic users
06
Chapter Six

The Digital Ecosystem:
RI.HIP & The Road to Robotics

The third most important conversion driver named by surgeons in the Voice of Customer interviews — scoring 9.0 out of 10 — was technology and 3D planning capability. S+N already has a comprehensive answer. POLAR3 is not just an implant system. It is the only hip construct in FraBeNe that pairs proven registry-leading survivorship with a full digital continuum of care — and a robotic future already in motion.

TODAY
The RI.HIP Solution — A Continuum of Care for Total Hip Arthroplasty

The RI.HIP ecosystem addresses the three phases where hip arthroplasty outcomes are most at risk — pre-operative planning, intra-operative execution, and post-operative learning. All three tools are available today, fully compatible with POLAR3 and OXINIUM, and require no preoperative CT scan.

PRE-OP
TraumaCad◊ Digital Templating
Provides surgeons with accurate implant sizes and a pelvic tilt assessment feature for adjusted anteversion in cup placement. Entry-level digital tool that integrates seamlessly into existing workflows without requiring new hardware.
PRE-OP
RI.HIP MODELER◊
Mobile (iPad) application. Before surgery, evaluates cup placement based on a patient's unique spinopelvic condition. Assesses implant impingement and other factors to reduce the potential for hip dislocation. No additional hardware required — runs on a standard iPad.
INTRA-OP
RI.HIP NAVIGATION◊
Comprehensive navigated solution for total hip arthroplasty. Delivers patient-specific component alignment — maximising accuracy and reproducibility for cup placement, pelvic tilt control, leg length and offset measurement. Image-free technology: no CT scan required. Available on the Brainlab Kick platform (500+ hospitals globally) and the CORI Surgical System. Small OR footprint; designed for both hospital and ambulatory surgery centre use.
POST-OP
RI.INSIGHTS◊
User-friendly web application. Dashboard allows surgeons to analyse case performance and metrics. Apply data insights and learnings from past cases to improve and refine upon the next case. Turns every procedure into institutional learning — a tool that gets more valuable over time.

RI.HIP NAVIGATION enables surgeons who perform total hip arthroplasty to execute a personalised surgical plan. This innovative technology combined with the OR3O Dual Mobility System helps to address hip instability.

Dr. Thorsten Seyler — First RI.HIP NAVIGATION procedure on CORI, Duke University, 2022
Navigation Clinical Evidence — Computer-Guided vs Conventional THA
1.06%
10-year revision rate with computer-guided THA using S+N implants vs 3.88% conventional (p=0.005)
55%
Lower risk of revision at 10 years with computer-guided vs conventional THA (p=0.038)
↑ SAT
Significantly higher patient satisfaction in the computer-guided group vs conventional THA (p=0.003)
Source: Davis ET et al. JBJS OA 2021. Study using S+N implants with computer-guided navigation vs conventional THA.
TOMORROW
CORIOGRAPH & The CORI Hip Vision — AI Planning and Robotic Execution
CORIOGRAPH◊ — FDA Cleared December 2024

CORIOGRAPH Pre-Op Planning and Modeling Services is now cleared for total hip arthroplasty. Exclusively for CORI Surgical System users, this AI-powered planning software directly addresses what multiple surgeons in our field interviews said they needed to move their score of S+N up by two points.

Built on 15+ years of clinical expertise and over 350,000 image-based hip and knee surgery plans, CORIOGRAPH provides:

AI-Powered Patient-Specific Planning
Advanced modelling using patient-specific spinopelvic classification and pelvic tilt — going beyond simple range-of-motion to simulate activities of daily living. Surgeons can assess impingement risk for each patient before the case.
Image-Agnostic — X-Ray or CT
Supports both 2D X-ray and 3D CT-based planning. Works across the entire spectrum of S+N hip implant solutions including POLAR3 — no system lock-in required for pre-operative planning.
Seamless Intra-Op Integration
When used with RI.HIP NAVIGATION on CORI, the pre-operative plan guides cup placement, leg length and offset intra-operatively — closing the loop between planning and execution.
CORI Hip — The Robotic Direction

The CORI Surgical System — S+N's next-generation handheld robotic platform, already established in knee arthroplasty — is actively expanding into hip. RI.HIP NAVIGATION is now available on CORI, and the robotic capability for hip is developing along the same trajectory that made CORI the leading handheld solution for knees.

Key advantages of the CORI platform for hip that surgeons should know now:

Smallest Footprint in Class
Designed for today's crowded operating rooms and the growing ambulatory surgery centre market. No dedicated OR suite required — it goes where the surgeon goes.
No Preoperative CT Required
Image-free smart mapping eliminates the time, cost and radiation exposure associated with preoperative CT imaging — a significant practical advantage over bulky robot-linked systems like MAKO.
Independent — Not Implant-Locked
Unlike MAKO, which locks surgeons into the Stryker implant ecosystem, CORI is compatible with the full S+N hip portfolio. The surgeon keeps clinical autonomy. This is the argument that breaks the "we're stuck with MAKO" objection.
Europe Momentum — CORI on Tour 2025
In May 2025, S+N launched a months-long roadshow across Germany, Austria and Switzerland — dozens of surgical facility stops demonstrating CORI for hip and knee. CE marking secured for European markets. The platform is moving.
How to Sell the Technology Roadmap — Today

You do not need to wait for full robotic hip launch to use this story. The technology argument is a conversion tool available right now — and it directly addresses the three surgeon profiles most likely to be unmoved by implant data alone.

For the surgeon asking about AI planning
"CORIOGRAPH is already FDA cleared for hip — built on 350,000 plans, image-agnostic, patient-specific spinopelvic modelling. This is the AI planning capability you described. And it works with POLAR3."
For the MAKO-locked surgeon
"CORI gives you planning precision and navigated execution without locking your implant choice to a single brand. When your MAKO contract comes up for renewal — and it will — what would you want your options to be?"
For the surgeon starting with navigation
"RI.HIP NAVIGATION is already in 500+ hospitals globally on the Brainlab Kick platform. No CT, small footprint, available now. And the data shows a 55% lower revision risk vs conventional THA. That's not a future promise — it's a current clinical outcome."
The bridge from knees to hips
"If you're already using CORI for knees, RI.HIP NAVIGATION is an upgrade path on the same platform — same system, same learning curve, same team. You already have the infrastructure."

The surgeon who partners with S+N on POLAR3 today gets access to the most complete hip digital ecosystem available — and the robotic future is being built around the same implants they are already using.

Power of One · Technology Positioning

07
Chapter Seven

Clinical Evidence:
The Complete POLAR3 Compendium

This chapter consolidates the full body of clinical evidence for POLAR3 — drawn from the POLAR3 Clinical Evidence Compendium (Smith+Nephew, February 2024, Version 6). Over 20 independent studies, four national registries, and ODEP ratings covering more than 650,000 documented procedures. This is your clinical arsenal.

Why the evidence context matters

THA has been described as the 'operation of the century' — one of the most cost-effective and consistently successful orthopaedic surgeries. National registries report approximately 95% 10-year survivorship for uncemented THA across the NJR, AOANJRR, and LROI. NICE recommends a revision rate of ≤5% at 10 years.

Top-performing constructs achieve as low as 1.9% — a 62% lower relative risk than the NICE benchmark. Revision THA costs 1.4× a primary procedure, with 1.5× longer hospital stays and 3× the re-revision risk. Implant choice materially changes these outcomes — and POLAR3 is uniquely differentiated.

Metric Value Benchmark / Comparator Source

POLARSTEM/R3 — 10-yr revision rate 1.93% 57% lower than class avg; 62% lower than NICE benchmark NJR 20th Annual Report 2023
POLARSTEM — 13-yr survivorship 95.9% 38% lower revision risk vs all other uncemented stems (p<0.001) NJR ISR, November 2023

08
Chapter Eight

Game On:
POLARSTEM vs CORAIL

CORAIL is the incumbent. It carries 35 years of clinical heritage and 2.5 million implants worldwide. To convert CORAIL surgeons, you need to understand exactly where POLARSTEM is superior — and how to make that argument land.

Feature POLARSTEM ◊ CORAIL ®
Proximal Body Width ~20% wider — enhanced proximal loading & stability Narrower — risk of metaphyseal underfill
Taper Angle Greater by 1° — stronger self-locking press-fit Standard taper — compaction broach only
HA Coating Thickness 50µm — optimal for osseointegration, low delamination risk 155µm — thicker; higher delamination / fatigue fracture risk
Coating Base Layer 180µm VPS Ti-Plasma Spray — doubles surface area, promotes bone in-growth even if HA resorbs Grit-blasted only — no redundant fixation layer
Broach Design Bone cutting teeth M/L + compaction A/P — cortical alignment + cancellous preservation Compaction grooves only — no cortical cutting
Medial Cortex Alignment Medial margin aligned to cortex — resists subsidence Narrow body may not achieve medial cortex contact
10-Year Revision Rate (NJR) 1.93% (95% CI: 1.57–2.36) 4.01% (95% CI: 3.89–4.13)
ODEP Rating 10A★ Stem / 13A★ Cup (R3) Not available for direct comparison
OXINIUM Compatibility Yes — access to best-in-class bearing technology No — locked to standard bearing options

The class-leading HA-coated cementless stem is widely considered to be CORAIL. But based on available registry performance data at 10 years — shouldn't that title belong to POLARSTEM?

Smith+Nephew Game On — Competitive Selling Guide
The Challenge/Response Playbook vs CORAIL
Their claim: "Bone preserving philosophy"
Your challenge: How does the CORAIL stem achieve both primary and secondary stability in compacted cancellous bone without cortical contact? The narrow proximal body underfills the metaphysis — raising the risk of rotational instability and subsidence.
Their claim: "Proven fixation via HA"
Your challenge: With 155µm of HA, what happens if the coating delaminates? Explanted CORAIL cases have documented HA coating loss. POLARSTEM's 50µm HA sits on a 180µm Ti-Plasma base — so if HA resorbs, osseointegration continues.
Their claim: "Standard of care"
Your challenge: POLARSTEM/R3 demonstrates a 1.93% revision rate at 10 years from 27,529 NJR cases vs 4.01% for CORAIL/PINNACLE from 190,860 cases. If you were designing your standard of care today, which system would you choose?
Part Three The Playbook
09
Chapter Nine

Voice of the Surgeon:
Field Intelligence & Practical Engagement Guide

Based on structured Voice of Market interviews conducted across Belgium, France and the Netherlands · Feb–May 2026. Every theme, every objection, and every recommended response on the pages that follow came directly from the field — from surgeons currently using Stryker, DePuy, Zimmer, and Medacta systems.

Surgeons change implants because they trust someone — not because they read a brochure. Your job is to earn that trust — one conversation, one case, one relationship at a time.

Surgeon Profiles — Field Interview Cohort
Surgeon Setting Current System Approach Annual Volume
Surgeon AMajor regional hospital — BelgiumStryker (MAKO robotic)Various / MIS~400 cases
Surgeon BUniversity-linked hospital — BelgiumStryker (MAKO) + partial S&N stemDAA & posterior~270 cases
Surgeon CCommunity hospital — BelgiumDePuy (Corail / Pinnacle)Not specified~175 cases
Surgeon DRegional public hospital — FranceZimmer (Avenir) + Cerf (Novae cup)Not specified~100 cases
Surgeon EPrivate clinic — FranceMedacta (Versafit / Amistem)Anterior (DAA)~100 cases
Surgeon FGroup clinic (private network) — FranceDePuy Corail + Cerf dual-mobility cupPosterior~90 cases
Surgeon GTeaching hospital — NetherlandsZimmer Biomet (Taperloc / Continuum)Posterior~150 cases
Six Key Themes
What every surgeon told us — and what it means for you
1
Theme One

Training Origin is the Primary Lock-In.

The single most consistent finding across all interviews: surgeons use what they were trained on during residency. This is not inertia — it is deep-rooted clinical confidence built over years of repetition with the same kit, the same technique, the same results.

What Surgeons Said
Surgeon F — France
"Experience. I have known it for many years." On why he still uses the same stem after a decade in practice.
Surgeon A — Belgium
"Experience from the past — you start with what you were trained with." On why he uses Stryker despite acknowledging the S+N portfolio.
Surgeon C — Belgium
"Habit and service." His two-word answer to why he stays with Corail/Pinnacle.
Surgeon D — France
"These are implants I saw placed during my residency. They are reliable and I have technical support available."
How to Engage
Opening Move
Open every first visit by asking: "Can you walk me through your training background and how you arrived at your current implant?" This shows respect and gives you critical intelligence. Never lead with product features — lead with curiosity about their surgical journey.
Break the Barrier
Prioritise getting them to see or handle the instrumentation. A cadaver lab, OR visit, or live demonstration breaks the psychological barrier of the unfamiliar faster than any conversation. Until a surgeon has physically handled the POLAR3 ancillary kit, no clinical study will move them.
Peer Validation
A local colleague who already uses POLAR3 and is trusted by the target surgeon will move them further in one conversation than months of sales visits. Activate your peer network before every conversion attempt.
2
Theme Two

Technology is Reshaping the Landscape — But Not Uniformly.

Robotics and digital planning are the decisive battleground in Belgium. In France and the Netherlands, surgeons are watching closely but are not yet locked into robot-linked implant systems. The approach you take must be tailored to where each account sits on this spectrum.

Belgium — The MAKO Problem

In Belgium, robotic lock-in is real. Several surgeons interviewed in Belgium are deeply committed to the Stryker MAKO ecosystem — not because they prefer the implant, but because their entire hospital infrastructure and commercial financial models are built around MAKO.

"The main barrier is robotics and the agreements with Stryker. They changed the entire OR layout to accommodate MAKO. Finance is very important — we are stuck now because of the financial model constructed around it." — Surgeon B, Belgium
France — Curiosity Without Lock-In

French surgeons interviewed are not robotically locked in for hips. Several are planning CORI acquisition for knee applications but do not see hip robotics as an immediate priority. However, they are vocal about the need for intelligent digital planning tools.

"What Stryker does really well is AI-powered 3D planning. You input the scan, and immediately AI gives you the right stem, the right plan. That is something important." — Surgeon E, France
How to Engage on Technology
Belgium — MAKO-Locked Accounts
Do not compete on hardware. Acknowledge MAKO's value, then reframe: "If you had access to the same planning precision without being tied to a single implant brand, what would that change?" The 3D planning gap within MAKO was named as a frustration — lean into it.
Belgium — CORI Users
This is your active opening. Position CORI + POLAR3 as the independent-minded surgeon's combination. A surgeon in the Belgian cohort confirmed that 3D planning is the #1 current gap with CORI — this is a live, named opportunity. Ensure every surgeon knows POLARSTEM and R3 are fully CORI compatible.
France — All Accounts
Lead with digital planning as a topic before leading with the product. Ask about their current planning workflow and where friction exists. Surgeons here are hungry for this conversation and it differentiates you from every other rep who leads with a brochure.
3
Theme Three

Every Surgeon Has an Unmet Need. Find It.

Even surgeons who described themselves as content with their current system identified specific gaps when asked directly. These unmet needs are your entry point — not a reason to attack the competitor, but an invitation to start a targeted, relevant conversation about POLAR3.

Unmet Needs by Surgeon Profile
SurgeonCurrent SystemGap / Unmet Need Identified
Surgeon AStrykerFemoral revision stem considered outdated. 3D planning independence from robotic system.
Surgeon BStryker + partial S&NCeramic-on-ceramic monoblock for small cups. 36mm head only from 54mm R3 — wants earlier access for smaller patients.
Surgeon CDePuyNo specific gap named — needs a full portfolio demonstration to be engaged.
Surgeon DZimmerNo cemented stem option for coxa vara patients. Wants closer size increments in the range.
Surgeon EMedactaNo valgus stem available (S+N has this). POLARSTEM perceived as too long for DAA. No dedicated DAA table solution.
Surgeon FDePuy + CerfOpen to switching the cup as part of group purchasing. Concerns about acetabular cup holder through small incisions.
The Most Important Question in Hip Sales
"Is there anything missing in the implant you currently use?" — Ask this even when you expect the answer to be no. One surgeon initially said he had everything he needed, then within the same conversation identified a clear opening on the cup. It works every time.
Map the Gap to POLAR3
Valgus stem option vs. Medacta · OXINIUM/XLPE for younger patients in ceramic cases · complete R3 size range · PolarCup for group purchasing frameworks. Know which POLAR3 feature answers which unmet need before you walk in.
DAA Surgeons — Address It Head-On
The anterior approach is a specific competitive sensitivity. Know the S+N DAA offering, the table solution status, and be transparent about the roadmap. Never try to talk around it.
4
Theme Four

Clinical Evidence and Complete System Are the Top Conversion Drivers.

When asked to rank what would actually convince them to switch implants, surgeons across all interviews consistently placed clinical evidence and a complete implant system at 9 or 10 out of 10.

Importance Scores — Averaged Across Field Interviews
Evidence / ActivityAvg /10What Surgeons Said
Clear clinical evidence9.3"10/10 — that is what would convince me." | "With OXINIUM, I would give it 8 — compelling."
Complete implant system9.2"For me, you have a perfect range." | "Has to run as smoothly as what I use today."
3D planning / technology*9.0"Would give it a 9 or 10." | "AI planning is something important." *Added unprompted by surgeons.
Intra-operative support8.8"10/10 — your team is perfect." Note: autonomous French surgeons rated this lower (1–3).
Training8.5"Reps present, education before cases, cadaver course, OR team training — all of it."
Surgeon development programme7.8"8/10 — needs to be a real plan, not a generic programme."
Environmental impact6.2"A bonus but not my decision criterion." Rising importance in institutional settings.
Commercial viability / hospital5.8"Surgeon has final say even if purchasing prefers lower price." Matters more in Belgium.
Engagement in clinical research4.8"2/10 — I do not see real engagement for surgeons." Lower in private practice.
Three Numbers Every Rep Must Know by Reflex
1.93%
POLARSTEM/R3 revision rate at 10 yrs (NJR) — 57% below class average. Your lead number vs CORAIL (4.01%).
4.9%
OXINIUM/XLPE cumulative revision at 15 yrs (AOANJRR) — lowest of all bearing combinations.
99.1%
POLARSTEM all-cause survivorship at 10 yrs (multicenter). Zero cases of stem subsidence.
Use Data with Precision
Before any conversion visit, prepare one data point from the POLAR3 compendium that is directly relevant to that surgeon's practice profile. Not all the data — one powerful, targeted insight. Never present data without context. Always link it to the surgeon's specific concern: younger patients, DAA cases, revision risk, long-term commitment to a single patient.
Complete System is Non-Negotiable
Before you discuss clinical data, confirm you can demonstrate a complete, consignment-ready system. A surgeon who asks "can I get this next week?" and hears hesitation is unlikely to convert.
5
Theme Five

Decision-Making Dynamics Differ Across Belgium, France and the Netherlands.

Understanding who to convince, in what order, and how long it takes is as important as understanding what to say. The interviews revealed clear structural differences across all three markets.

DimensionBelgiumFranceNetherlands
Decision-makerSurgeon has final say — but must align with hospital committee and purchasingIndividual surgeon almost entirely autonomous. Group purchasing frameworks (Vivalto/PEP) increasingly influential in private clinic groupsSurgeon drives clinical preference but purchasing committees (Inkoop) and hospital value committees carry significant weight. Centralised procurement increasingly common in larger networks
Key gatekeepersMedical Material Committee (CMM), purchasing, pharmacy. All three must be engagedPharmacy (stock/consignment approval). Group purchasing for clinic networks. IBOD / scrub technicians carry strong informal influenceInkoop (procurement), Medical Devices Committee, zorgverzekeraar (insurer) frameworks in some accounts. OR coordinator influence significant
Change timeline4–14 months from commitment to first consignment1–3 months for a trial · 3–6 months for full consignment2–6 months for trial · 6–12 months for full consignment in larger hospitals
What purchasing needsFull pack: clinical data + economic justification + logistics + training + service commitmentPrimarily logistics reliability. Price is secondary to clinical preference in most casesStrong emphasis on health economic value, DBC compatibility, and registry data. LROI data particularly persuasive — Dutch surgeons respect Dutch registry outcomes
Influence networkNational surgical societies. A small number of high-volume trusted colleagues. One influential surgeon can unlock a hospitalTrusted local peers carry most weight. Regional colleagues who have used POLAR3 are decisiveAcademic centres (UMC networks) set the standard. Strong peer influence within regional orthopaedic networks. NOV (Dutch Orthopaedic Association) congress visibility important

"If one or two surgeons in a hospital have high enough volume — 200 cases — you do not need the rest of the group to get the product in. You just need the right surgeon."

Surgeon B — Belgium
Belgium — Map the Influence Network First
Who sits on the CMM? Who does the target surgeon trust? Find the high-volume advocate and build from that relationship outward. Map this before your first conversion conversation.
France — Speed is Your Advantage
When the French surgeon is ready, the decision cycle is short. Focus energy directly on the surgeon, then move fast to prepare pharmacy and logistics — this is where French conversions slow down or fail.
Netherlands — Lead with LROI Data
Dutch surgeons trust Dutch data. The LROI (Landelijke Registratie Orthopedische Implantaten) is their reference point — make sure you know how POLAR3 performs in LROI data and lead with it. OXINIUM's strong registry performance across the NJR and AOANJRR also resonates. Academic centre surgeons and UMC networks set the standard others follow — prioritise these accounts early.
Group Purchasing Accounts (France)
PolarCup is an identified low-disruption entry point — it allows you to start building volume without requiring a full system conversion from day one. Identify which implants sit within the network's preferred framework.
6
Theme Six

S+N is Trusted — But Not Yet Known in Hips.

Brand perception findings are consistent across all interviews: Smith+Nephew is trusted, experienced, and associated with quality. But primarily in knees. In hips, the brand is either neutral or associated with good-but-not-innovative. This is a challenge and an opportunity simultaneously.

How Surgeons Described Us — Verbatim
Surgeon B — Belgium · Rating: 9/10
"Excellent implants, good track record, flexible company, dynamic, listens to surgeons. You make custom trays — most companies are far more rigid. But we need to make more noise as a company."
Surgeon A — Belgium · Rating: 7/10
"We consider you one of the big players — but I do not find the portfolio sexy."
Surgeon E — France · Rating: 8/10
"The first word that comes to mind is experience. A serious, recognised company."
Surgeon F — France · Cannot rate hips
"Knees — always. I have little experience with your hip implants."
What Would Move the Score +2 Points — In Surgeons' Own Words
Named by multiple surgeons
3D planning capability and AI-assisted surgical planning
Named as the single most decisive step
A full, hands-on portfolio demonstration — "show me the system"
Named by DAA-specialist surgeons
A credible anterior approach solution with a dedicated table
Brand positioning
Greater visibility and noise — congress presence, peer education, making the hip story as loud as the knee story. Use the NJR 1.93% data as a brand statement to reframe S+N in hips from 'the knee company' to a data-backed hip leader.
The Conversion Playbook
Stage-by-stage — know where you are and what to do next
Four Stages. One Approach for Each.

Every surgeon you visit fits into one of four conversion stages. Identifying the stage before you arrive determines your objective, your opening question, and your proposed next step. Never walk into a call without knowing which stage you are in.

StageProfileField SignalsYour Objective
Stage 1
Unaware		 Associates S+N with knees only. Has never seen or handled POLAR3. Surgeons C, F (hips) Portfolio demonstration visit. Bring the kit. Show OXINIUM/XLPE. Leave the compendium.
Stage 2
Curious		 Knows S+N has a hip range. Has an unmet need. Has not yet engaged seriously. Surgeons D, E Arrange a peer visit to a current user. Cadaver lab if possible. Open the first-case conversation.
Stage 3
Evaluating		 Actively considering a trial. Has identified a specific entry point (cup, case type, patient profile). Surgeons D, F (PolarCup) Launch First 10 Cases programme. Full logistics and training pack. Set a date.
Stage 4
Committed / Blocked		 Would convert but is locked by robotics, contracts, or hospital politics. Surgeons A, B (MAKO) Long game: maintain relationship. Plant seeds on 3D planning gap. Prepare for contract renewal.
The First 10 Cases Programme — What Surgeons Said They Actually Need

Multiple surgeons were asked directly what would make a First 10 Cases programme acceptable. Their answers were consistent, specific, and actionable. Everything below came from the interviews — not from internal assumptions.

StepTimingAction
14–6 weeks beforePeer reference visit or call. Surgeon speaks with a current POLAR3 user in a comparable practice.
23–4 weeks beforeFull instrumentation presentation to surgeon + key OR staff. Ancillary layout, sizing logic, technique review.
32–3 weeks beforeOR team education session — nurses, scrub tech, sterilisation. Kit walkthrough and sterility protocol confirmed.
42 weeks beforeConsignment set confirmed in pharmacy. RIZIV (BE) or equivalent compliance confirmed in writing. Case booked.
51 week beforeOptional cadaver session if technique change involved. Case planning review with rep.
6Case dayExperienced rep on-site for full case. Immediate post-operative debrief. Capture any friction points.
7Cases 2–5Rep present. Adjust based on feedback. Address OR team concerns proactively.
8After case 10Formal review: surgeon + OR team feedback, logistics assessment. Define path to routine use.
Practical Conversation Guide

Language You Can Use — Drawn Directly from the Field

Opening a Conversion Conversation

"I want to make the most of your time today. I'm not here to pitch — I'm here to understand your practice better and be honest about where we might genuinely fit. You can be completely direct with me. What are the two or three things you care about most when you think about your hip implant choice?"

Suggested opening — use as a starting point, not a script
Handling "I'm Happy with What I Use" — The Most Common Response

"I completely understand — and if everything were perfect, there would be no reason to change. Just out of curiosity: is there anything you find yourself working around? Anything where you have to adapt your technique because of a limitation in the current system?"

Response framework — then listen. Every surgeon named at least one limitation.
Positioning Clinical Evidence — By Surgeon Profile
For a surgeon focused on longevity / younger patients
"In the NJR — across more than 500,000 uncemented hip constructs — POLARSTEM with R3 had a 1.93% revision rate at 10 years. That is a 57% reduction versus the class average. For a 55-year-old patient in your practice, that difference is meaningful."
For a surgeon focused on bearing surface
"The OXINIUM/XLPE bearing has the lowest cumulative revision rate of all bearing combinations in the Australian registry at 15 years — 4.9%. From year one, it shows a 38% lower revision risk compared to standard metal/XLPE. That is not a marginal difference."
For a surgeon concerned about revision risk
"We have 10-year data showing 99.1% all-cause survivorship for POLARSTEM — with zero cases of stem subsidence and zero bone hypertrophy in any case. The radiographic picture is as clean as the clinical one."
When a surgeon raises 3D planning
"You are right — this is an area we are actively developing, and I want to be transparent about where we are. POLARSTEM and R3 are fully compatible with CORI, and our planning tools are evolving. What would it mean for your practice if you had access to AI-supported planning that was not tied to a single implant brand?"
Asking for the Next Step — By Conversion Stage
Stage 1 — Unaware
"Would you be open to 20 minutes with me and the kit — just to see the system? No commitment, just curiosity."
Stage 2 — Curious
"I can connect you with a colleague in a similar practice who is using POLARSTEM regularly. Would a short conversation with them be useful before we go further?"
Stage 3 — Evaluating
"Let's pick a first case together. I'll make sure everything is in place — the set, the training, the team preparation. What case type feels most natural to start with?"
Stage 4 — Blocked
"I understand where you are — and I'm not asking you to change anything today. What I'd like is to stay connected so that when the timing is right, we are ready. Can we check in quarterly?"
Quick Reference
The essentials — ready for your next call
Five Numbers. Know Them by Heart.
Data PointNumberWhen to Use It
NJR 10-year revision rate (POLARSTEM/R3)1.93%
57% below class avg		Against any uncemented stem. Your lead number vs CORAIL (4.01% at 10 years in NJR).
AOANJRR 15-year revision rate (OXINIUM/XLPE)4.9%
Lowest of all bearings		When bearing surface is the conversation. Superior to ceramic-on-ceramic and ceramic-on-XLPE.
OXINIUM vs. Metal/XLPE revision risk38% lower
From year one (p<0.001)		When surgeon evaluates bearing choice or raises concerns about metal sensitivity or longevity.
POLARSTEM 10-year survivorship (multicenter)99.1%
Zero stem subsidence		When surgeon wants long-term confidence. No cases of stem subsidence or bone hypertrophy.
OXINIUM revision rate in patients under 551.80%
Lowest for this age group		For active or younger patient populations — a direct differentiator from ceramic options.
The Questions That Opened Every Door
QuestionWhy It Works
"Is there anything missing in the implant you currently use?"Works even with satisfied surgeons. Surfaces unmet needs without threatening the status quo. Opens every conversion conversation.
"Who would need to be convinced first if you wanted to change implant?"Maps the influence network in a single question. Tells you exactly who else to call on.
"What would it take for your score of us to go up by 2 points?"Surfaces specific asks and gives you a concrete action agenda. Shows you are listening and serious.
"If you were to do a trial with us, what would have to happen between now and then?"Gets the surgeon to articulate the path themselves. Converts vague interest into a real, owned conversation.
"Whose opinion do you trust most on hips in your region?"Identifies the peer network and opinion leaders. Enables precise ambassador activation.
Pre-Visit Checklist — Every Hip Call
☐ Know the surgeon's current implant system and surgical approach
☐ Know their approximate annual primary hip volume and revision split
☐ Identify which of the four conversion stages they are in
☐ Prepare one specific POLAR3 data point relevant to their patient profile
☐ Know the local peer network: who do they trust, who uses POLAR3 nearby?
☐ Know the hospital decision-making structure: committee, purchasing, pharmacy
☐ Have a proposed next step ready before you walk in — specific, concrete, achievable in the next 4 weeks

The surgeon who feels understood is the surgeon who is open. The surgeon who is open is the surgeon you can convert. Every conversation is a chance to be the rep who actually listened.

Chapter Ten · Part Three
The Pre-Call &
In-Call Workbook
Prepare smarter. Perform better. Leave with a next step every time.

This chapter is your field execution toolkit. Each section is designed to be used in sequence — before, during, and after every hip sales call. The templates are built directly from the surgeon intelligence gathered in Chapter 9 and the conversion framework throughout this book. Print them, fill them in, and bring them to every call.

1
Section One

Surgeon Profile Sheet

Complete one per surgeon before your first conversion visit. Update it after every call. This is your single source of intelligence for each account — keep it current and refer to it every time.

Surgeon / CMM / Purchasing
Key Gatekeepers to Engage

POLAR3 user they trust nearby

BE: 4–14 months · FR: 1–6 months
Technology / Robotics Status
MAKO / CORI / None / Interested
Intelligence Notes / History
2
Section Two

Pre-Call Planner

Complete this before every hip call. Never walk in without knowing your objective, your evidence, and your proposed next step. Five minutes of preparation changes the conversation.

Surgeon / Account

Current Stage
Call Type
First visit / Follow-up / Trial prep

Second likely objection:

3
Section Three

In-Call Reference Card

Keep this open during the call. It is not a script — it is a prompt sheet. Use it to stay oriented, deploy evidence precisely, and handle objections without losing momentum.

Opening Questions — Choose One Based on What You Know
Surgeon TypeOpening QuestionWhy It Works
First visit / Unaware"Can you walk me through your training background and how you arrived at your current implant choice?"Shows respect. Gives you critical intelligence. Opens the conversation on their terms.
Satisfied / Content"Is there anything in your current system you find yourself working around?"Works even with satisfied surgeons. Surfaces unmet needs without threatening the status quo.
Technology-focused"Where does friction exist in your current planning workflow — before the case, in the OR, or after?"Opens digital/planning conversation. Differentiates you from every rep who leads with a brochure.
MAKO-locked"If you had access to the same planning precision without being tied to a single implant brand — what would that change for you?"Reframes the robotics conversation. Plants the seed without attacking MAKO.
Evaluating / Near trial"If you were to do a trial with us, what would have to happen between now and then?"Gets surgeon to articulate the path themselves. Converts vague interest into a real conversation.
Live Objection Handler
ObjectionThemeResponse
"I'm happy with what I use"Theme 1"I completely understand. Just out of curiosity — is there anything you find yourself working around? Any limitation you've had to adapt your technique for?"
"CORAIL has 35 years of data"Ch8 / Game On"Absolutely — and it's a well-established system. In the NJR at 10 years, POLARSTEM/R3 shows a 1.93% revision rate vs 4.01% for CORAIL/Pinnacle. If you were designing your standard of care today, which system would you choose?"
"We're locked into MAKO / Stryker"Theme 2"I understand — and I'm not asking you to change anything today. When does your current arrangement come up for renewal? CORI gives you navigated hip execution without implant lock-in. Worth knowing your options."
"S+N is a knee company"Theme 6"That's exactly what I want to address. Did you know POLARSTEM/R3 has a 1.93% revision rate at 10 years in the NJR — 57% below the class average? You already know what it means to work with us. Let me show you what that looks like in the hip."
"I need 3D planning / AI tools"Ch6 / Tech"CORIOGRAPH was FDA cleared for hip in December 2024 — built on 350,000 plans, image-agnostic, patient-specific spinopelvic modelling. This is the AI planning capability you're describing. And it works with POLAR3."
"POLARSTEM is too long for DAA"Theme 3"That's a valid concern and I want to be direct about it. Let me show you the sizing range and the DAA instrumentation — including the dedicated add-on tray and curved impactor. I'd also like to connect you with a DAA colleague who's using it regularly."
"The committee / purchasing decides"Theme 5"Absolutely — and I have a full conversion pack covering clinical, economic, logistics, training and service. Who else should I be speaking with? If you're on board clinically, I want to make sure we have everything ready for the right conversation with the right people."
Evidence Deployment — Match to Surgeon Profile
Longevity / Younger patients
"1.93% revision rate at 10 years in the NJR — 57% below class average. For a 55-year-old patient, that difference is meaningful."
Bearing surface focus
"OXINIUM/XLPE: 4.9% cumulative revision at 15 years in the Australian registry — lowest of all bearing combinations. 38% lower from year one."
Revision risk concern
"99.1% all-cause survivorship at 10 years for POLARSTEM — zero cases of stem subsidence. Zero bone hypertrophy. The radiographic picture is as clean as the clinical one."
Under-55 / Active patients
"OXINIUM revision rate in patients under 55: 1.80% at 10 years — lowest for this age group. A direct differentiator from ceramic in your most demanding cases."
Technology / Navigation
"Computer-guided THA with S+N implants: 1.06% revision at 10 years vs 3.88% conventional. 55% lower risk. Higher patient satisfaction. Available today on CORI."
Complete system / Reliability
"ODEP 10A★ stem. ODEP 13A★ cup. One instrumentation set. One training. Compatible with every surgical approach. Everything you need to run a full hip programme."
Closing Language — By Conversion Stage
Stage 1
UNAWARE
"Would you be open to 20 minutes with me and the kit — just to see the system? No commitment, just curiosity."
Stage 2
CURIOUS
"I can connect you with a colleague in a similar practice who uses POLARSTEM regularly. Would a short conversation with them be useful before we go further?"
Stage 3
EVALUATING
"Let's pick a first case together. I'll make sure everything is in place — the set, the training, the team. What case type feels most natural to start with?"
Stage 4
BLOCKED
"I understand where you are — and I'm not asking you to change anything today. Can we agree to check in quarterly? When the timing is right, I want us to be ready."
4
Section Four

Post-Call Debrief

Complete within 30 minutes of every call — while the conversation is fresh. This feeds your account playbook, your pipeline tracker, and your next pre-call planner.

Surgeon / Account
Date
Stage Before
Stage Now
Was the call objective achieved?
Yes — fully

No

Must be specific — not "follow up". What action, by whom, by when?
Date Agreed
5
Section Five

Account Pipeline Tracker

One row per target account. Review this at every bi-weekly pipeline call. If a surgeon has been in the same stage for more than six weeks without a concrete next step, escalate or reframe the approach.

Surgeon / Account Country Current Implant Stage
(1–4)		 Unmet Need / Entry Point Last Contact Next Step Next Step Date Trial / Conv. Target
Stage 1 — Unaware

Has never seen POLAR3. First visit not yet made.

Stage 2 — Curious

Engaged. Unmet need identified. Peer visit pending.

Stage 3 — Evaluating

Trial in planning. Entry case identified. Date needed.

Stage 4 — Blocked

Willing but locked. Maintain, monitor contract renewal.

Preparation is not bureaucracy. It is the difference between a rep who reacts and a rep who leads. The surgeon notices — even when they don't say so.

S+N
